identity.research_question: This project integrates tCycIF, single-cell, and TCGA detection. This project is a collaboration with Vähärautio Lab.
identity.project_summary: This project aims to explore the role of KRAS in tumor progression and immune microenvironment modulation by integrating tCycIF, single-cell, and TCGA data with wet lab experiments. We focus on identifying KRAS-associated cellular states, their spatial distribution, and interactions between epithelial and immune cells. Key findings will be validated through functional assays, including the detection of KRAS amplification.

Suitable for the problem: Is there a significant spatial interaction Build a spatial adjacency graph (usually based on the distance between cell centroids, such as k-nearest or radius-based). For a specified cell type pair (A-B), calculate the number of neighbors in the real data (i.e. the number of A adjacent to B). Use the permutation test to shuffle the cell type labels multiple times and then calculate the number of neighbors to form a null distribution. # **Project Overview**

This project aims to explore the role of KRAS in tumor progression and
immune microenvironment modulation by integrating tCycIF, single-cell,
and TCGA data with wet lab experiments. We focus on identifying
KRAS-associated cellular states, their spatial distribution, and
interactions between epithelial and immune cells. Key findings will be
validated through functional assays, including the detection of KRAS
amplification.

## Slide 11
UTAG: unsupervised discovery of tissue architecture with graphs
To identify and quantify microanatomical tissue structures from multiplexed imaging data.

Multiplexed imaging data types are typically imaging mass cytometry (IMC), co-detection by indexing (CODEX), multiplexed ion beam imaging by time of flight (MIBI-TOF), cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF), and others.

It can reveal organ-specific domains and structural differences between healthy and diseased tissues, offering valuable insights into processes (such as tumor development or immune responses)

GitHub: https://github.com/ElementoLab/utag

## Slide 12
UTAG: unsupervised discovery of tissue architecture with graphs
Based on expression data
annotation not required
It needs 2 matrices as input:
Expression matrix
Adjacency matrix: just 1 or 0
Next, to multiply them
2 paremeters:
Max_dist: Threshold euclidean distance
Resolution: leiden method
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41592-022-01657-2

## Slide 2
Panel of markers used in tCycIF
Functional biomarkers

13. γH2AX: A marker of DNA double-strand breaks, widely used as a functional marker for DNA damage response

## Slide 4
RCN
Recurrent Cellular Neighborhood

Refers to clusters of spatially organized cells that are defined by their cell types (more stable classifications based on cell lineage and differentiation)

By mapping spatial positions and cell types within the tissue, RCN analysis quantifies the interactions and co-localizations of different cell types within a defined radius, capturing recurring spatial patterns of cell identity.
RPD
Recurrent Phenotyping Domain

Refers to clusters of spatially organized cells that share specific phenotypes (distinct expression profiles based on marker expression, indicating functional or activation states)

By mapping spatial positions and marker expression within the tissue, RPD analysis quantifies the interactions and co-localizations of different phenotypes within a defined radius, highlighting consistent functional or state-based patterns.

## Slide 5
Differences between methods
Scimap

Based on location and cell types.

The main focus is on the classification of cells and their spatial distribution relationship.

Problems that tend to be solved:
Spatial relationships between cell populations: Interaction patterns of different cell types, such as whether immune cells tend to gather around certain specific types of cancer cells.

Functional connections between tissue structure and cell types.
To solve different problems
UTAG

Based on location and marker expression.

Instead of relying on a priori cell classification, it’s performed directly based on the protein marker expression pattern of each cell.

Problems that tend to be solved:
Spatial relationships of cell states: the activation or inhibition in th tumor microenvironment.

Identify microenvironment features: whether a microenvironment with high expression of certain proteins is more inclined to attract specific cell types.
Which types of cells are near each other, and whether these relationships are related to certain functions or pathologies.
How do expression patterns vary between cells, and how do changes reflect underlying biological functions.

## Slide 9
Scimap spatial interaction
Purpose: Count whether the frequency of interaction between two cell types in tissue space is significantly higher than the random distribution.
Suitable for the problem: Is there a significant spatial interaction

Principle:
Build a spatial adjacency graph (usually based on the distance between cell centroids, such as k-nearest or radius-based).

For a specified cell type pair (A-B), calculate the number of neighbors in the real data (i.e. the number of A adjacent to B).

Use the permutation test to shuffle the cell type labels multiple times and then calculate the number of neighbors to form a null distribution.

Compare the real neighbor count with the random distribution to obtain the p-value and effect size (z-score).

## Slide 12
Purpose: To measure the physical distance between a certain type of cell and other cell types, how close they are.
Suitable questions: Who is closer to whom?

Principle:
Build a spatial adjacency graph.

For each cell, test whether they are interaction.

It calculates two distinct scores:
Proximity Density: 
Total number of interactions identified divided by the total number of cells of the cell-types that were used for interaction analysis.
Provide insight into how frequently these cell types interact relative to their population size.
Proximity Volume:
Total number of interactions identified devided by the total number of all cells in the data.
Provide a broader view view of the interactions’s significance across the ectire sample.

Usually no permutation test is involved, and the "degree of proximity" is mainly given descriptively, without necessarily significant comparison.

## Slide 22
Scimap proximity score
Define the proximity between specified cell types within spatial data. It calculates two distinct scores:
Proximity Density: Total number of interactions identified divided by the total number of cells of the cell-types that were used for interaction analysis.
Proximity Volume: Total number of interactions identified divided by the total number of all cells in the data.

Limitation
Each circle with certain radius as a whole, and the condition is met as long as there are A and B in it. 
However, in many biological hypotheses, we are more concerned with "whether there are B cells around A cells" rather than "A and B exist in a mixed area at the same time."
Cannot give us directionality: Cell A is always next to cell B, but cell B is not always next to cell A.

Conclusion
Scimap proximity scores can give us idea how close of 2 phenotypes, but it will miss direction

COZI is one updated method to calculate spatial interaction, it will calculate conditional z score, combined with permutation.

I think we can use COZI, but we need to think how to combine with our exact project, for example, if we want to compare the differences between 2 tumor sub-type.

## Slide 4
Copy Number Variation Analysis
Protocol: Dragen Copy Number Variant Calling
Output:
segment_mean
segment_mean = log2(observed depth / expected depth)
~ 0 → Normal (copy number 2)
~ -1 → Deletion (copy number ~1)
~ +0.58 → Duplication (copy number ~3)
To refer CNVs from read depth.
copy number
The absolute copy number assigned to a genomic segment.
2 = Normal diploid copy number (i.e., no CNV).
0 = Homozygous deletion (complete loss of both copies).
1 = Heterozygous deletion (loss of one copy).
3+ = Copy number gain or amplification.

Non-parametric test
It makes fewer assumptions about the data — it doesn’t assume a specific distribution (like normal distribution).
Instead, it often uses ranks of the data rather than the actual values. 

Not sensitive to the extreme values, because only the sign and rank of the difference are used, it doesn’t focus on the mean.

Sample size requirement: can use small size (5~30)
Parametric test
It makes assumptions about the parameters of the population distribution from which the samples are drawn — normal distribution.

Tests whether the mean difference between paired samples is zero.

Sensitive to the extreme values, because we will calculate mean and standard deviation using raw differences.

Sample size requirement: usually greater than or equal to 30 to be stable

Only keep Malignant cells

Patient-Level Aggregation (Pseudobulking)
Calculate Medians: Group the data by patient_ID and KRAS_state, computing the median UCell score for each pathway per patient. 
Statistical Inference (Two-Sample Bootstrap)
Define Comparisons: KRAS Amp vs. Neutral, or KRAS High [Amp+Gain] vs. Neutral
Robust Bootstrap Method: 10,000 iterations.
Resampling: For each iteration, independently resample patients with replacement within each group

rence (Two-Sample Bootstrap)
Define Comparisons: KRAS Amp vs. Neutral, or KRAS High [Amp+Gain] vs. Neutral
Robust Bootstrap Method: 10,000 iterations.
Resampling: For each iteration, independently resample patients with replacement within each group
Effect Size: Calculate the difference in medians between the two resampled groups.
Confidence Intervals: Construct a 95% Confidence Interval (CI) from the empirical distribution of the 10,000 median differences. A pathway is considered significantly altered if its 95% CI strictly excludes zero.

## Slide 9
WES
FGA (Fraction of Genome Altered)
To observe genome instability (GIN)
excludes Copy-Neutral LOH

Method 1: Standard FGA (Baseline = 2)
Definition: segments with CN_total ≠ 2
Compares all samples to a normal diploid cell baseline
Formula: FGA = sum of altered segment lengths / total genome length
To observe the distance between genome of tumor cells and normal cells

Method 2: Ploidy-adjusted FGA
Dynamically sets baseline for each sample based on ASCAT ploidy
Adjusted baseline = nearest even integer to ASCAT ploidy value
Formula: FGA = sum of segments with CN_total ≠ adjusted_baseline / total genome length
To observe how many times the genome has altered based on its existing ploidy during tumor evolution.
To see the rate of tumor evolution and its drug resistance potential.

Cytoskeleton / migration / adhesion | KIF5B, CAP1, FSCN1, TES, CLDN1, LAMC2, LAMB3, CTNNA1 | These encode actin-binding and adhesion proteins, indicating increased motility and invasive behavior. LAMB3/LAMC2/CLDN1 upregulation is often seen in epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT).
Vesicle trafficking & translation control | AGO2, EIF4G1, EIF5B, EIF5, STAU1, ASAP1/2, CLINT1 | KRAS activation often reprograms RNA metabolism and translation, consistent with increased expression of translation regulators and RNA-binding proteins.
Oncogenic co-factors | MALAT1 (lncRNA), YWHAZ (14-3-3ζ), MTDH (Metadherin) | MALAT1 and MTDH are cancer-associated; YWHAZ regulates many oncogenic signaling proteins including RAF and AKT.

## Slide 75
DEG – breakpoint of Chromosomo 12
This plot shows the logFCs of genes across Chr 12, here I used 'changepoint' package of R to detect the breakpoints automatically.
Changepoint segmentation was based on logFC ordered by chromosome position, here Chr12 was divided into 8 regions with relatively consistent mean value of logFCs.

The 22.0–27.0 Mb region had the highest mean logFC (≈3.4), corresponding to KRAS and its neighboring genes, suggesting that this region is a core amplification region related to KRAS.

The upstream and downstream regions of 14.8–21.6 Mb and 27.2–32.7 Mb also showed moderate upregulation, constituting a broader amplification region.

## Slide 76
DEG – breakpoint of Chromosomo 12
Method = "PELT" --> Pruned Exact Linear Time
It's the most commonly used and fastest algorithm for changepoint
Its logic is as follows:
Scan the entire sequence from left to right
At each point, calculate "if this is a breakpoint, is the cost of the segments before and after it smaller"
Use "BIC" penalty to avoid generating too many dotted lines.

changepoint
Its logic is as follows:
Scan the entire sequence from left to right
At each point, calculate "if this is a breakpoint, is the cost of the segments before and after it smaller"
Use "BIC" penalty to avoid generating too many dotted lines.
Use "pruning" to discard candidate points that are unlikely to be the optimal breakpoint in advance
Finally, it returns: the breakpoint that minimizes cost+penalty
